data.title: GDS Holdings: AI Boom Fuels Rally, But Monetization Risks Remain (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
GDS (NASDAQ:GDS) shares have more than doubled since our last note (see: GDS Holdings: From AI Darling To Cash-Burning Nomad) as investors become bullish on China’s AI play in the wake of DeepSeek announcement that it could deliver a comparable AI model at a fraction of the cost relative to that of overseas peers.

The partnership between Alibaba and Apple to provide AI services in China also fueled the AI rally, implying that Alibaba could ramp up AI and data center spending to meet the rising demand for Apple AI services in China.

All these factors position GDS as one of the beneficiaries, given that Alibaba is one of the largest clients for GDS.

We are changing our rating of GDS to Hold from Sell as we believe that the risk and reward are balanced. We need further evidence on domestic stabilization to be more comfortable with the stock.

On the upside, we believe that the emergence of DeepSeek and the rising adoption of DeepSeek applications by companies across several industries may have initiated an AI and data center capex cycle that should benefit GDS, given its hyperscale exposure.

On the downside, we believe AI service monetization in China remains questionable and that CSPs' AI and DC investments could pull back if monetization does not materialize. Even if monetization kicks in, the proliferation of similar AI models in China could drive downward pricing pressure that may also moderate this capex growth cycle.

With valuation trading at 16.5x forward EBITA, we believe GDS is fairly valued relative to its global peers. Much of the expectations surrounding future growth from Alibaba and Tencent seem to have been baked into the stock price. We recommend that investors take profit at the current level until there is solid evidence of domestic demand stabilization before making this stock a long-term holding.

Two factors that are making us change our bearish view:

First, DeepSeek has become a catalyst for major AI and DC spending among cloud service providers, and this cycle could be sustainable in the foreseeable future.

According to industry estimates, China’s data center capex is expected to reach $10bn this year and grow at an annual CAGR of 9.2% to $17bn by 2031 driven by large CSPs ramping up on cloud infrastructure. BABA’s latest quarterly result indicated that FCF declined significantly due to a sharp increase in capex, most likely related to data center spending.

Peers Tencent and Baidu have also ramped up or are on the verge of ramping up data center spending. At the same time, ByteDance plans to allocate more than $20bn in data center capex in 2025, with half allocated to overseas spending.

With China aiming to become a global leader in AI by 2030, we have seen AI become a strategic national priority similar to that of EVs, green energy, and semiconductors.

The emergence of DeepSeek underscores that an alternative AI is possible in China that could rival that of its overseas peers. Rising capex in the Chinese context is likely to drive a new wave of the capex cycle, and this is likely to benefit GDS given its exposure to hyperscalers.

Second, Alibaba’s partnership with Apple and Tencent’s integration with DeepSeek could drive higher data center demand.

BABA’s recent partnership with Apple to develop AI features for iPhone users in China is likely to benefit GDS in that the incremental AI demand from the estimated 200 million iPhone users in China necessitates a substantial increase in computational resources and data storage, which will lead to an increase in the utilization of GDS data centers to support the AI workload. Given that utilizing the excess capacity in China remains a priority for the GDS management, we believe this could alleviate some of the challenges that GDS is facing and could result in higher monthly service revenue.

Tencent’s integration of DeepSeek AI into its Wechat platform is also relevant to GDS.

By incorporating DeepSeek's AI capabilities, Tencent is enhancing its service offerings, which is expected to attract more users and increase data traffic. We see Tencent’s move as a counter to the BABA-Apple partnership in that Tencent is looking to enhance the AI capability of its leading software to counter the potential competition from Apple AI in China. With Tencent expected to increase its investment in AI and data center, we believe GDS could be the key beneficiary as it seeks a more scalable solution for The only risk we are cautious of is the uncertain monetization nature behind AI in China, which ultimately impacts the CSPs and the data center providers such as GDS. We note that the current growth narrative is that GDS has benefited from the AI boom as major CSPs like Alibaba and Tencent are expanding their AI-driven cloud infrastructure.

However, if CSPs cannot sufficiently monetize the AI services, then there is a high likelihood of reduced investment in infrastructure spending. Worth noting that the Chinese market has been a difficult place to get users to pay for any software. Microsoft has been having this issue since entering China and still faces this challenge even today. Therefore, if AI monetization does not materialize, CSPs could cut back on AI-focused capex spending, leading to lower growth for GDS.

Equally important is to remember that the current pricing environment for data centers in China remains very competitive, with MSR for GDS continuing to decline due to competition and overcapacity. If CSPs fail to charge users for AI services, they may negotiate lower pricing with data center operators like GDS, leading to a decline in unit economics and profitability erosion. We do not rule out the scenario in which the proliferation of AI models in China results in a hyper-competitive race to the bottom environment, similar to that of EVs and coffee, resulting in industry-wide margin erosion.

With GDS shares trading in-line with its global peers at 16.5x forward EBITDA, we believe the valuation looks fairly priced at this level and recommend investors to either hold or take profit given that the upcoming quarter will likely result in another narrative in which domestic MSR remains weak and that we could see further deceleration in China revenue.

From the fund's most recent prospectus. Net expense ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver/expense reimbursement through 09/30/2025, unless sooner terminated at the sole discretion of the fund's board.

Fund performance

Columbia Flexible Capital Income Fund Institutional Class shares returned -0.67% in the fourth quarter ending December 31, 2024. The fund’s return finished in line with the -0.60% return for its blended benchmark.

Market overview

The financial markets generated mixed returns in the fourth quarter to close out a generally positive year. The November presidential election result removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors, and it raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction. In addition, U.S. equities continued to attract investor capital as the nation’s economic growth remained well above that of its global developedmarket peers. However, these developments also caused investors to ratchet down the number of interest-rate cuts the U.S. Federal Reserve was expected to enact in 2025.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Top holdings (% of net assets) as of December 31, 2024

1.02
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

This backdrop provided a tailwind for equities, but much of the positive return continued to come from the same narrow group of mega-cap, technology-related companies that led the way through the first nine months of the year. Returns for most stocks finished well behind the broad-based indices, and market breadth was relatively poor. These conditions created a headwind for the value style relative to the growth style and for dividend payers versus the market as a whole. As a result, the fund’s holdings in equities lost ground as a group. Positions in several health care companies — including CVS Health Corp. (CVS), Amgen (AMGN), and Medtronic PLC (MDT) — detracted. Real estate investment trusts also posted losses at a time in which Treasury yields rose sharply.

On the other hand, positions in a number of financial stocks contributed positively. The fund held a number of winners in the capital markets and large-cap banking areas, led by Morgan Stanley (MS) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM). A position in the semiconductor producer Broadcom (AVGO) was an additional contributor of note. The stock staged an impressive rally behind strong earnings and expectations that the company is poised to capitalize on the growth of Artificial Intelligence.

The fund’s fixed-income holdings produced a negative return as a group. Bonds, in general, weakened in the fourth quarter due to the unfavorable shift in the outlook for U.S. Federal Reserve policy. We continued to find opportunities in the fixed-income space, as weakness in Treasuries translated into increased yields across the corporate bond market. Relatively stable issuers are now offering much more attractive coupons than they have in a number of years, allowing the fund to generate attractive portfolio income without taking on excessive risk.

The fund’s holdings in convertible securities gained ground and helped offset some of the shortfall in other areas. The healthy return for convertibles reflected the combination of investors’ appetite for risk and yield, as well as the category’s above-average sensitivity to the growth style. Chart Industries (GTLS), which makes containers used in liquid natural gas transportation, was a key contributor. The growth in this business has translated into robust cash flows for the company, together with a reduction in its leverage. Bloom Energy (BE), a producer of backup power sources used by data centers and other large enterprises, was another sizable contributor, thanks to new contracts it signed during the quarter. Positioning in financials also contributed, led by Apollo Global Management (APO). On the other hand, convertible holdings in the information technology and utilities sectors detracted.

Outlook

We continue to have a positive outlook on the sectors in which the fund invests. We believe a blend of dividend stocks, convertibles and fixed-income investments can participate in potential market upside if the broader investment environment remains supportive. At the same time, we think the portfolio can provide a measure of downside protection if unforeseen factors lead to higher market volatility than we have witnessed in recent years. Along these lines, we note that the fund provides a differentiated option for those who are looking to diversify away from the mega-cap technology stocks that drive an ever increasing portion of individual investors’ portfolio returns. We believe this may prove to be a compelling attribute if financial-market leadership begins to broaden in the year ahead.

Investment Risks

Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments,

including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. Non-investment-grade(high-yield or junk) securities present greater price volatility and more risk to principal and income than higher rated securities. Convertible securities are subject to issuer default risk. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments held by the fund, negatively impacting its performance and NAV. Falling rates may result in the fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering the fund’s income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. The fund may also be forced to convert a convertible security at an inopportune time, which may decrease the fund’s return. Value securities may be unprofitable if the market fails to recognize their intrinsic worth or the portfolio manager misgauged that worth. Foreign investments subject the fund to risks, including political, economic, market, social and others within a particular country, as well as to currency instabilities and less stringent financial and accounting standards generally applicable to U.S. issuers. Risks are enhanced for emerging market issuers.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. For a free prospectus or a summary prospectus, which contains this and other important information about the funds, visit columbiathreadneedleus.com. Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Columbia funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA and managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC.

Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Additional performance information: All results shown assume reinvestment of distributions and do not reflect deduction of taxes that a shareholder would pay on fund distributions or the redemption of fund shares.

Bloomberg Index Services Limited. BLOOMBERG® is a trademark and service mark of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates (collectively “Bloomberg”). The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a market-value-weighted index that tracks the daily price, coupon, paydowns and total return performance of fixed-rate, publicly placed, dollar-denominated, and non-convertible investmentgrade debt issues with at least $250 million par amount outstanding and with at least one year to final maturity.

The Flexible Capital Income Blended Benchmark is a weighted custom composite consisting of 1/3 Russell 1000 Value Index, 1/3 Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade & High Yield Index and 1/3 Bloomberg U.S. Convertible Composite Index.

data.title: Great Time To Short IONQ (And Other Quantum Computing Stocks)
data.description:
Thesis

I have written about the company several times and shorted the stock several times. However, the technical picture is always important to pay attention to. Right now, an attractive combination of high valuation and some technical aspects signals a short opportunity. In my last article in December 2024, I didn't mention shorting the share because it was still too much in an upward trend. However, a few days ago, I started a short position. The share price seems to follow the typical "bubble stages chart" pattern.

Quick recap

I don't want to repeat much in this article that I've already written, so here's just a brief summary. The company's CEO says that quantum computers could soon have a ChatGPT moment, and who knows, he might even be right. My argument is mainly that this company's visibility of potential sales and technological leadership is unclear. In other words, even if quantum computing is soon technically ready for a wide range of uses, the company is still competing with much more financially strong competition. Usually, only a few companies succeed, while most go bankrupt. That said, it is not at all clear yet how much real-world use quantum computing really will have.

If they want to deliver to data centers very soon, why is there so little precise information in the investor presentation? One of my biggest criticisms is that future revenue potential is pure speculation, given the current state of knowledge. I don't see indications that allow a profound analysis, and the company also doesn't predict much about its future.

My last article: IonQ: The Tide Lifts All Boats, But Here It's Not Justified

Why the good run in quantum computing stocks?

Nowadays, it is not easy to explain why charts move the way they do. There is a lot of trading by algorithms, institutional investors, and ETFs. Private investors probably only make up a tiny fraction of the movements. In small markets such as quantum computing, launching an ETF may result in all stocks in the sector are performing pretty much the same. But what I know is that one pattern often repeats: almost identical movements for different stocks in the same sector. The similarity below is obvious.

What happened around November 2024 that caused the prices of all shares to suddenly rise?

Specifically for IONQ, there was the announcement of a "$54.5M contract with U.S. Air Force Research Lab". I think the news about some new patents and the acquisition of Qubitekk may have contributed less to this. IONQ has also raised its guidance for 2024, which the market strongly appreciates.

However, this does not explain the good performance of the other shares like Rigetti Computing (RGTI). The broader uptrend likely happened due to Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) announcement of a new quantum chip. This news was published on Seeking Alpha on December 10, 2024 ("Quantum stocks accelerate on Google's Willow chip."), and strongly correlates with the uptrend in the charts above. Paradoxically, a success story from a direct competitor has caused a rise in the small quantum computer stocks. That never made sense to me. Google is a strong competitor to these smaller companies. However, similar events occur again and again, most recently a few days ago, when a Honeywell R&D center was announced.

Honeywell shares rose 1.3% following the news, while other quantum computing stocks rose 10% or more, including IonQ (NYSE:IONQ), Quantum Computing (QUBT) and D-Wave Systems (QBTS).

News: Quantum stocks keep shining as Honeywell's Quantinuum announces new R&D center

Still, only based on hype

Although this hype has led to strong share gains in a short time, nothing has changed fundamentally: Quantum computers are still very niche, and overall, a lot of research is going on with many different players involved. This means heavy competition for the limited talent. Additionally, like in most areas, there is heavy competition not only from Big Tech but also from China. Furthermore, there is little visibility of the long-term sales outlook or hard, quantifiable facts about the usefulness of quantum computing.

The CEO of IONQ made a statement about $1B in sales in 2030. However, after researching several articles, I have to say that I see little solid evidence of where this figure should come from.

Early Friday, IonQ's Chairman and CEO Peter Chapman said he expects bookings for the quantum computing company to be at the "high end" of its forecast, and forecast revenue could approach $1B by 2030.

Seeking Alpha: Quantum stocks slide after D-Wave CEO refutes Nvidia's Huang

But okay, I don't claim to know more than the company's CEO, so let's assume these sales estimates will materialize. Even then, the company's current market capitalization is already a multiple of that: it is currently valued at a market cap of $7.34B.

So, even if sales amount to $1B in 2030, we are still talking about a price-to-sales ratio of 7.3 at the current valuation. At the same time, we have no information about whether the company would be cash flow positive at all; and, if so, at what margins. As I said, I think that's pretty much the best-case scenario. And this is even based on the current number of outstanding shares, which will likely increase significantly by 2030.

These are the analysts' earnings estimates for the coming years. Even if these come true and the CEOs' 2030 statement is also correct, I believe this still does not justify the current valuation.

The technical picture indicates the bubble is bursting

This chart should always be remembered, especially as a shareholder in high-risk stocks. The entire cycle has already been mirrored in numerous examples of past hype stocks. It is great to be part of the hype on the upside, and there's nothing wrong with that, but it is even more important to get out in time.

The parallels between this "bubble stages chart" and the actual IONQ chart are astounding. We can see the phases of enthusiasm and greed, and if we continue to follow the chart above, the phases of fear and capitulation will soon follow.

The share is still above the 200-day average, indicating a long-term upward trend. However, it recently fell below the 50-day average, which could signal a momentum reversal in the short to medium term or, in other words, the weakening of the bullish long-term trend.

The volume also supports this. During the bullish phase, there was a strong increase in volume. However, during the declining stock price in recent weeks, volume has decreased as well. This could indicate that buying interest is decreasing.

The RSI signals that the stock is currently neither overbought (above 70) nor oversold (below 30). The trend confirms the loss of the stock's upward momentum. But keep in mind that the lower the RSI, the more likely a rebound.

Overall, it is important to consider technical signals in the context of other factors, such as fundamental analysis. In this case, the combination of the bearish RSI, the drop below the 50-day moving average, and the declining volume suggests, in my opinion, that an overall bearish phase may be imminent.

Risks to my thesis

Of course, every reader must remember that I could also be wrong. For example, one risk for my thesis is that it may be too late to take a short position. I built up my short position a few days ago, and in the meantime, the RSI index, for example, has fallen even further. The further it falls, the more likely a rebound in the other direction could come.

Understand the risk of shorting

It is essential to understand that as a short seller, you have potentially unlimited risk of loss. In addition, you pay a fee to borrow the shares. Exactly how much varies from company to company and also changes over time. This should be checked before a short sale is made.

I like to work with stops, especially when it comes to volatile stocks like this one, and also to not lose potential temporary gains again. Typically, I set a trailing stop loss at around 20%. So, if I short-sell at $10 and the stock rises to $12, I would get stopped out. However, if the stock falls to $8, the stop loss would trail, and I would get stopped out at $9.6 in that case. This is my personal preference, but everyone should decide for themselves.

Conclusion

Given the valuation, I believe the share still has much downside potential, and the technical picture has started to look rather bearish, too. If these two aspects come together, I think a stock represents an attractive short opportunity. My bearish thesis is based not only on the technical picture but, in the long term, primarily on fundamental analysis: I can see no compelling arguments as to how the company can prevail against much stronger competitors such as Google.

data.title: Brown-Forman: Pessimism Gone Too Far
data.description:
Sometimes share prices of high-quality and well-run businesses fall significantly not due to mismanagement issues or problems with the company's strategy, but rather because of macroeconomic or industry-wide headwinds.

In the latter scenario, it is far easier to conceive a bullish investment thesis and I believe that Brown-Forman Corporation (NYSE:BF.A) (NYSE:BF.B) stock now falls firmly within that category.

Over the past 3 years or so, Brown-Forman's stock has fallen by nearly 60% which is also the case for its two major peers - Diageo (DEO) and Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PRNDY).

This clearly illustrates that there is a common detonator that's weighing on returns within the sector. While the severity of the problems faced by each of the three companies do vary, they could be summarised as demand headwinds, inventory overhangs, costs, tariffs and even changes in exchange rates.

Even though these are all legit concerns for investors, as they could have a profound impact on both sales and margins, they all become less of an issue, the longer investment horizon one has.

Before jumping on the bandwagon, however, we should:

  • be aware of the ongoing risks and their implications for Brown-Forman's business both in the short and the long term;
  • fully understand how the stock is priced-in at the moment and what this means for investors' expectations.

Market Scepticism

The narrative around Brown-Forman has worsened significantly in recent months as the company is once again caught in the midst of a trade war.

As trade relations between the U.S. and the European Union continue to deteriorate the latter is poised to reinstate tariffs on exports of American spirits at a 50% rate in March.

On a geographical basis around 6% of Brown-Forman came from Germany in FY 2024, 3% from France, 1% from Spain and 3% from Poland. Combined this makes roughly 13% of sales and with the rest of the EU markets the total impact of the EU block is likely to be no more than 20% of sales.

So far it appears that reintroduction of tariffs on American whiskey by the United Kingdom might be avoided, but the bad news is that Canada and Mexico also falling within the scope of the ongoing trade war. Additionally, it appears that India - a small market for Brown-Forman that is also growing, has reduced tariffs on bourbon whisky to 100% from 150% previously. In China, however, things are going in the opposite direction.

Understandably, investors are quite worried at the moment given the very dynamic situation in terms of trade negotiations and taking a wait-and-see approach seems like a sensible thing to do. However, with nearly 50% of Brown-Forman's sales coming from its home market, the company appears to be in a far better situation to some of its peers.

The 12% reduction of the company's workforce is also quite telling when it comes to the management's expectations about future demand and to make matters worse the company will incur severance charges of around $60m-$70m, which is around 5% of EBIT.

So far, it appears that headwinds for Brown-Forman have somehow dissipated with the company reporting flat sales for the first half of FY 2025, after adjusting for divestments. As a matter of fact, it appears that the company's premiumization strategy is going well with Woodford, Diplomatico and Old Forester brands doing well.

Our reported net sales decreased 5% in the first half, while organic net sales were flat after adjusting for the divestitures of Finlandia and Sonoma-Cutrer in the prior fiscal year, the negative effect of foreign exchange and the recent business model change for Jack Daniel's Country Cocktails. In the first half, an increase in used barrel sales was the most significant growth contribution to organic net sales. From a brand perspective, Woodford Reserve, Diplomatico Rum and Old Forester were the largest growth contributors to organic net sales.

Source: BF.B Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

The iconic Jack Daniel's brand is also performing well in recent months.

After several quarters of short-term headwinds, I'm very pleased to say that organic net sales for Jack Daniel's Tennessee Whiskey accelerated significantly as we move through the first half of fiscal '25 with sales now flat for the year-to-date period.

Source: BF.B Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

Based on all that it appears that the analysts' expectations of revenue staying flat over the coming years are already taking into account demand headwinds resulting from the ongoing trade war with Europe, Canada and Mexico.

With that in mind, we should evaluate whether or not the market is accurately pricing-in these headwinds or if the stock price has gone down way too much in recent years.

Pessimism Gone Too Far

Just for some context, BF.B is now trading at the same levels that it was back in early 2014. If we exclude dividends, the stock has returned 6% over the past 11 years which speaks volumes about market expectations.

While revenue has been falling slightly since FY 2023, it is still up by roughly 30% since FY 2014.

Net income, on the other hand, was below $700m in FY 2014 and is now above $1bn for the past 12-month period.

One more thing that we should consider is that since calendar 2014, equities more broadly have experienced a massive tailwind on the back of increased liquidity and favourable financial conditions. For comparison, the Shiller P/E ratio has gone up from slightly above 24 in 2014 to nearly 38 as of today. If this wasn't the case, then BF.B share price would have been deeply into negative territory over the past 11-year period.

Another way to look at Brown-Forman's share price is in relative terms. Usually, slow-growth companies in the consumer staples sector exhibit a very strong relationship between their price/sales multiple and the operating margin on a historical basis.

This, however, is no longer the case for BF.B as the current P/S multiple of 3.6 is at one of the lowest levels in more than 20 years, while at the same time operating margin remains near record highs. As we could see on the graph below, this is a drastic change from the 2018-2023 period when BF.B was priced at a premium relative to its operating profitability.

While tariffs will inevitably take their toll on Brown-Forman's profitability, it is highly unlikely that the EBIT margin would fall below 20% and stay there. The full impact of the trade war is still to be determined, but so far, the outlook for FY 2025 is for adjusted operating margin to remain flat with both net sales and operating income expected to increase within the 2% to 4% range.

Conclusion

It appears that Brown-Forman's share price has gone down way too much to justify even a challenging 2025. While uncertainty regarding the ongoing trade war is making investors' nervous, the pessimism has gone way too far. The scenario that is currently priced-in appears highly unlikely and, in my view, this is a rare opportunity to buy a highly profitable and stable business at a significant discount.

data.title: Apple: I Was Wrong (Technical Analysis)(Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Apple, Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL)(TSX:AAPL:CA) on November 28th, 2024 with my article “Apple: Potential Breakout Ahead”, the stock was attempting to form a base after falling to lows of $219.71 during the November 4th trading session. At the time, I felt that we were starting to see a fairly significant increase in the number of criticisms being levied against AAPL long positions based on weaknesses seen in Apple’s China sales figures. In my view, those criticisms missed several key bullish arguments that had the potential to send share prices to new all-time highs in a relatively short period of time. To be fair, we did ultimately see the stock rally by more than $25 in less than one month following the publication of my prior coverage. However, the dramatic reversal from those new record highs took me (and likely many other Apple investors) by surprise and the size of those declines has forced me to reassess the probability that Apple stock is likely to succeed as a stand-out member of the Mag-7 in 2025. As a result, I admit I was wrong, and I have closed my AAPL long position and will be downgrading my outlook to a “hold” rating based on the idea that I think there are more attractive mega-cap tech stocks to be found elsewhere.

First, the clear positives in the latest earnings report must be acknowledged because gross margins were quite impressive at 46.9% and this helps make clear to the market that strength in Apple’s services business is making up for disappointing weaknesses in broader iPhone sales growth. Given these record-high figures in Apple’s gross margin, I would argue that the seemingly constant focus on iPhone sales alone fails to address the ways the company is adapting and evolving in a more globally competitive environment. In other words, revenue growth in advertising, AppleCare technical support, payment services, and purchases from the App Store have allowed the company to overcome one of the central bearish thesis items. Furthermore, the company expects to see second quarter gross margins to rise 47% based on the company’s guidance range for the period (46.5% - 47.5%), so it is not as if this latest quarterly performance is likely to just be a “flash in the pan.” In Q1, revenue from the services segment came in at $26.3 billion (indicating growth rates of 4%) and now makes up more than 20% of the company’s total revenue figure. Of course, all of this progress builds on the $100 billion revenue milestone that the services segment achieved in Q4 last year.

However, when we look into the stock’s most important valuation metrics, Apple’s investment prospects start to look much less appealing. For example, Apple’s forward price-earnings ratio is currently seen at 33.51x. Of course, this is not as elevated as some of Apple’s Mag-7 counterparts because Amazon (AMZN) currently trades with a forward price earnings ratio of 35.26x and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) still towers over the rest of the group at 124.73x. However, there are still four other stocks that are trading at cheaper valuations even though Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) are trading in somewhat similar regions (at 31.62x and 31.56x, respectively). From there, however, it is fairly clear that Meta Platforms (META) at 27.52x and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) at 20.78x still maintain themselves as the most attractive stock selections amongst the mega-cap cohort.

Unfortunately, the situation is much worse if we look at Apple’s valuation from the perspective of the price-book metric at 55.32x. As we can see, there is no other stock in the Mag-7 cohort that is currently trading at higher levels. Nvidia is the closest in the group right now (at 52.05x) but the rest of these stocks really start to deviate sharply at this stage. Overall, Apple’s price-book valuation really started to accelerate into the upward direction during the first-half of 2024, but this is an issue that has been in place for quite a while. To highlight these differences, all we need to do is look at Alphabet (which is trading with a price-book valuation of just 7x). Even then, GOOG is not exactly a significant outlier because AMZN is seen at 8.26x and Meta Platforms is seen at just 9.638x.

From a chart perspective, I think it is important to identify the near-term support and resistance levels that are likely to influence trend activity going forward. Share prices recently overcame shorter-term resistance zones near $247.19, which is a bullish event on its own. Of course, this does suggest that selling pressure might be relatively limited as we head into the month of March 2025 and this is one of the main reasons I decided not to revise my outlook to a “sell rating.” However, upside potential from current levels does appear to be quite limited in my view, and this is why I would rather close my long positions in AAPL and focus on other (less expensive) Mag-7 stocks instead. Since the stock is still trading above its entire exponential moving average cluster, the lack of nearby resistance suggests that another test of the $260 level might be possible, and I would maintain a moderately positive stance on these timeframes.

Unfortunately, the picture becomes cloudier as we step back and look at things from the longer-term perspective. Here, we can see that the price history is starting to look extremely top-heavy, with momentum readings on the daily timeframes reaching over-extended levels. This is not the type of scenario I want to see if I am considering long positions in any stock because major resistance (the stock’s all-time high) rests less than 6% above current share prices (based on the close from the February 21st, 2025 trading session). If we do see share prices break above the $260.10 resistance zone, this would invalidate my stance and suggest that new bullish trend activity is currently developing.

We are also seeing a bearish harmonic pattern that is developing on this timeframe, and the downside price target that would be required for full completion of the pattern would be found at $216.98. In the chart, the formation that is shown is referred to as a Bearish Deep Crab pattern and is essentially based on a series of Fibonacci retracements that base trading decisions on contrarian trend activity. One potential issue with this pattern would be the fact that the stock did manage to bounce from the -2 Standard Deviation channel on the daily timeframe (which does qualify as a strongly bullish signal). Extensive backtesting that has been conducted with these patterns using historical data shows that, in a large number of cases, harmonic patterns will not reach their full point of completion. As a result, it is possible that this Bearish Deep Crab pattern has already reached its end-point (when share prices bounced from the -2 Standard Deviation during the January 21st trading session).

Ultimately, we will only see confirmation of this counter-argument if share prices move above $268.05 because this would invalidate the price pattern. Until (or unless) this occurs, I will hold-off from buying AAPL shares based on the expectation that downside risk currently outweighs upside reward potential. For all of these reasons, I will be lowering my rating outlook to a “hold” rating and I will be focusing more heavily on other members of the Mag-7 cohort that are currently trading at cheaper valuations.

data.title: Herbalife: The Battleground Stock Is Showing Some Turnaround Potential
data.description:
Herbalife Ltd. (NYSE:HLF) is a health and wellness company with operations in 95 nations through six million independent distributors in a direct selling and multi-level marketing model (or MLM for short). Its products include things like weight management, targeted nutrition, fitness, skincare, and body care items. Interestingly, HLF is the classic “battleground stock,” as there are strong arguments for both the bull and bear cases. Currently, HLF is indeed struggling with declining sales volumes in all its regions, signaling potential weakening demand or maybe market saturation. As it stands, HLF’s valuation seems to be pricing in the worst-case scenario. But its numbers in 2024 and Q4 have actually been quite favorable, suggesting a potential turnaround in 2025. So, I think HLF might make sense as a speculative “Buy” until a quarterly report invalidates this bull case.

Herbalife - A Battleground Stock

Herbalife is a multinational manufacturer and marketer of health and nutrition products. It actually has a long history since it was established back in 1980, and today it operates in 95 countries. For this, it uses the MLM business model, operating through a distributor network of six million independent members. Other than that, its product line is diverse, including products for weight management, nutrition, fitness, skincare, and body care.

However, at the core of this stock lies its MLM business model, which is what makes it unique from an investment perspective. HLF's business model is a combination of direct selling and multi-level marketing with independent distributors forming networks. The MLM portion of its business has encountered legal problems and intense scrutiny throughout the years, with the risk of being classified as a pyramid scheme.

In fact, in 2016, the US Federal Trade Commission [FTC] investigated the company. In my view, the outcome was actually quite positive for HLF, as it basically got a “slap in the wrist” with a $200 million settlement. Since then, HLF had to restructure its US business processes. In particular, HLF modified its incentives program to focus more on selling through retailing than on the recruitment of new distributors.

On top of that, the company implemented a system to verify sales. In other words, they now have to ensure their products are actually being delivered to legitimate customers outside of the distributor chain. This is key because the distinction between a legitimate MLM and a pyramid scheme can sometimes get blurry. So, I would argue that HLF has indeed adopted the right steps towards mitigating future potential legal issues on this front. And in the meantime, I think it’s fair to say that, indeed, many real people use the products themselves. This would be unlike a pyramid scheme, where most members simply join based on the “dream” sold to them.

Moreover, HLF has also stopped making income-potential promises in advertising, pulling ads that suggested a life of wealth by joining the distribution channel. There’s an Independent Compliance Auditor [ICA] who monitored the company's compliance with the terms of the settlement for seven years, reporting to the FTC. So, I think the business model itself is much more “sanitized” from that perspective, even though it does hamper their marketing efforts to some extent.

Now, if we look at their sales breakdown based on their different business segments, weight management is by far the most profitable, with 55.4% of total sales. This is followed by 29.7% of income from the targeted nutrition products. Energy, sports, and fitness account for 11.5% of net sales, and skin and body care 1.7%. The remaining profit comes from its literature and promotional divisions. So, I think it’s a relatively diversified set of revenue verticals within the concentrated bet on its distributor network. Nonetheless, HLF’s main product still is the “Formula 1 shake mix,” which is their best-selling meal substitute, representing 26% of overall sales.

Price Increases Versus Falling Sales Volumes

Interestingly, HLF has five key regions: North America, Latin America, EMEA, Asia Pacific, and China (reported separately). Each of the areas showed a different performance in the most recent 10-K report. One thing stands out for me, which is that even though overall revenues increased or stayed flat, there’s a clear decline in sales volume across all regions.

Fortunately, HLF has managed to offset lower volumes with increased pricing. However, while this might work in the short term, it does raise some questions about its long-term sustainability. After all, lower volumes might suggest a deeper problem like weakening demand or market saturation. In fact, we do know some join Herbalife solely to benefit from the product discount, so even with the pricing increases, their demand curve might remain favorable for HLF’s broader business. Basically, it’s possible that HLF indeed has more pricing power than initially anticipated. We simply can’t tell at this stage for sure, and that’s partly why 2025 will be a pivotal year for HLF.

For example, North America’s revenues declined by 6.8% while prices rose by 3%. In Latin America, the revenues increased by 1.4% due to rather modest growth in volume but a very significant price increase of 6.3%. EMEA’s revenues rose by 1.5% from a large 9.4% price increase to offset a decline of 7.1% in sales volume (note EMEA also got hit by a -2.9% impact from FX adjustments).

As for Asia Pacific, this is now HLF's biggest market, accounting for about 34% of the total sales. And this region grew by 0.6% with a strong demand for weight control products, even though it’s a bit constrained by regulatory limitations. This region also had a sales volume decrease of 0.3%, offset by a pricing increase of 3.5% (including a -2.4% FX impact as well). But the worst market is undeniably China at this point, as it suffered the sharpest volume decline of 9.1%, coupled with unfavorable FX adjustments and legal constraints on the direct-selling business model.

But at the same time, we’ve seen strong distributor and sales leader growth. New distributors increased by more than 22% YoY in Q4. Similarly, active sales leaders grew over 8% YoY in Q4. All while improving HLF’s retention metrics from 68.3% to 70.3%. Q4 even marked the third quarter in a row of YoY distributor growth, which also suggests this might be a more enduring trend (even though China remains a headwind). So, just looking at their adoption figures, I think there are clear signs of a potential turnaround in the making if they can sustain this momentum into 2025.

Strategic Changes And Trends

Product-wise, I think HLF is certainly making the right decisions. I particularly like the complement of GLP-1 medications they have. After all, GLP-1 drugs do reduce appetite and slow digestion, which can help with weight loss. This drug has some side effects, like muscle reduction, nutrient deficiency, and digestive problems. But I believe they make sense within HLF’s portfolio since they also have protein components that could help relief those side effects.

Since HLF members often grow small support communities focused on health and fitness, this can help them keep muscle mass, prepare meal replacements, and nutrient supplementation. Similarly, they can offer herbal aloe concentrate to improve gut health to counteract fatigue due to lower calorie consumption.

Nevertheless, direct-selling models face significant challenges not only because of the scrutiny and regulations surrounding their business model but also because digital channels are tricky to adopt effectively with this approach. Having said that, HLF is indeed investing in technology to help its members improve their online business channels. They now have platforms like MyHerbalife, HN MyClub, Engage, Bizworks, and Herbalife Hub.

But at the core of their business still remains the person-to-person relationships that each member has to cultivate to grow their business. And the new incoming CEO might have a new way of looking at online channels or might double down on the community aspect of HLF’s business. This is one of the main strategic uncertainties that I see at this point with HLF.

HLF Stock Valuation And Risks Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, I think HLF is actually quite clear. It trades at a $811.6 million market cap, and its guidance is for $600-$640 million in EBITDA for 2025. Additionally, they have approximately $2.3 billion in debt. Since they also have $415.3 million in cash, that means their EV is around $2.7 billion. If we assume a 2025 EBITDA midpoint of $620 million, that prices HLF at an EV/EBITDA of 4.4. This is self-evidently a very cheap valuation for almost any company.

However, the issue is whether or not HLF can sustain its current revenue levels for the long term. If that holds, then yes, I would argue that HLF is indeed cheap and a compelling investment. But if we continue to see a substantial decline in revenues due to an overly controversial or outdated business model, then it could be a value trap. Personally, I think the short-term trends of distributors and sales leaders are quite encouraging. And this has been a sustained trend in 2024, so it’s difficult to speculate that it will reverse in 2025.

Additionally, the FX impact on HLF has been negative in 2024. But it could very well become a tailwind in 2025, depending on what the Fed does this year. After all, if the Fed resumes rate cuts and the USD weakens as a result, then HLF’s FX impact should be favorable against the MXN, BRL, EUR, and CNY.

Sales volume remains a concern since it continues to decline, and China, in particular, will be a major variable this year. But after such a massive contraction in HLF’s China numbers, including FX impacts, it’s difficult to imagine it will just continue heading straight to zero. I think a more reasonable case would be to see the decline slow down and stabilize this year, while the other regions may even grow slightly. Similarly, pricing could further help offset volume declines. It’s unclear how much pricing power HLF actually has, but it could keep turning that dial for the short term until it hits a limit that becomes an issue.

Worth A Speculative “Buy”

Overall, I believe, on aggregate, HLF’s valuation is so depressed that it probably already prices in the worst-case scenarios for the variables I’ve outlined in this article. But conversely, I think there’s also a reasonable scenario in which HLF’s volumes stabilize or recover, FX impacts become a tailwind, distributor, and sales leader numbers continue to improve, and debt is reduced over time. So, on balance, I think HLF’s Q4 and full-year numbers show there’s a realistic potential for a turnaround in 2025. The new leadership remains a wildcard, but refreshing the management team could also work out. This is why I think HLF makes sense as a speculative “Buy” in 2025 until we see a quarterly report that disproves the turnaround bull case.

data.title: Ride The Fat Dividend Train, +10% Yield: Capital Southwest
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Being the youngest in my family, I always had the desire to measure up to or beat my older brothers. We were all skilled in very different ways. My one brother creates amazing art, something none of us could best. Eventually, we all found our natural rhythm and focused on our specific skill sets; the childish games of beating one another as a measure of success fell by the wayside.

In the market, often our competitiveness can get the best of us as well. Many investors feel they're only winning if they "beat the market" (SP500), as if that total return is arbitrarily the only way to measure success. However, the success I need and desire financially may not be the best way for you to meet your individual goals. A measuring stick is only useful if it adequately helps you measure movement toward your personal goals. Don't get caught up in childish competition and lose focus on what you're working towards.

This is a big reason why I am a massive advocate for actionable goals when it relates to your portfolio. Goals you can measure, have a time limit, and aren't vague or open-ended, like "I want to beat the market" or "I want to have $1,000,000". Pick something that you can measure that works and corresponds with your goals

Today, I want to dive into a company's earnings that many of my frequent readers will recognize as a top-notch income generator. It's been a key player in helping me achieve my annual income goals.

Let's dive in!

A Focus On Growing Its Portfolio

Capital Southwest Corporation (CSWC), yielding 10.1%, is a business development company, BDC, that lends to middle-market companies within the United States. Many BDCs focus on a niche section or approach to this mostly private realm of the market. Unlike many peers, CSWC focuses on the lower middle market companies - firms with $5 million to $50 million in annual revenue.

CSWC is giving us a little extra income this quarter, announcing the regular dividends as unchanged at $0.58, and announcing a supplemental dividend of $0.06. The supplement dividend last quarter was $0.05.

It is important to keep a perspective on what supplemental dividends are. When a company is differentiating between a "regular" and a "supplemental" dividend, management is telling you something. CSWC has paid a supplemental dividend for 10 quarters in a row. After such a long period, it is easy to get used to the supplement and just assume that it will continue indefinitely. It won't. That's why it is labeled as a "supplement". CSWC's business model is to borrow at fixed rates and lend at floating rates. As a result, CSWC has enjoyed a boost to earnings which is primarily due to high interest rates and the slow pace of the cutting cycle so far.

Management separates the dividend into two parts. The "regular" dividend is a dividend that we can expect to continue absent something unfavorable happening. It is an amount that management believes will be sustainable in a wide variety of interest rate conditions. The "supplement" is a distribution that relies on the current environment of above-average earnings.

CSWC has a great track record of producing excess earnings and paying supplements and special dividends. However, the supplement can disappear without anything going "wrong" with the company. Source

The dividend yield in the Model Portfolio includes the $0.06 supplement for this quarter, but it does not assume any supplement will be announced in future quarters.

Take supplements for what they are: CSWC is enjoying especially favorable conditions for their business and is sharing the excess with investors.

For the 3rd quarter (their FY ends March 31st), CSWC's earnings were very stable. CSWC's pre-tax NII came in at $0.64, exactly the same as last quarter. NAV at $16.59 unchanged from last quarter.

Non-accruals were at 2.7% of the portfolio, down from 3.5% last quarter. CSWC ranks their investments in four categories, with "1" being the strongest, suggesting companies that are outperforming underwriting expectations, and "4" being the weakest investments where management anticipates a loss is probable.

CSWC saw some material improvement in credit quality, with 9 loans upgraded and 3 loans downgraded:

CSWC has been taking advantage of its share price trading at a premium to NAV to issue equity. After a relatively flat year, CSWC increased the size of its portfolio last quarter to $1.78 billion, up from $1.6 billion the prior quarter.

In short, a rather flat quarter from the headline numbers, but CSWC saw improvements in non-accruals, credit quality and had more aggressive growth that will benefit future quarters. This is the first meaningful portfolio growth we have seen in about a year. Growing the portfolio will provide room for earnings growth that is sustainable even as the interest rate environment changes.

Conclusion

Among the realm of BDCs with stellar performance, CSWC is a top contender.

As an income investor with income as a primary goal - and portfolio value sustainability and growth as a secondary one - CSWC and its peers have provided strong and recurring income for a long time. I expect them to continue to do so.

This outpouring of income overwhelms my expenses and douses the flames of financial worry and anxiety. Retirement is a time to spend benefiting from all of the hard work that you have put in - that means exploring hobbies, traveling, or simply spending time with a loved one. If your portfolio is holding you back because of how you built it over the last decades, now is a perfect time to adjust that portfolio - so that it supports you in your retirement dreams instead of holding you back. If you're still working towards your retirement, make sure your portfolio is a tool that helps you leverage the best possible retirement ever, instead of simply having to be an albatross that you're feeding money into, hoping it gains value over time.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Very Bad News For Closed-End Funds
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

One of the main opportunities in closed-end funds comes from the discount/premium mechanisms that are naturally occurring in these investment structures. The share price can trade wildly different from the underlying net asset value per share. That can create some situations where there are some really good opportunities in these investment wrappers.

As a reminder, CEFs aren't an asset class or sector representation themselves. They are simply what their underlying portfolio can provide exposure to, which means they can provide some fairly diverse exposure. That includes equity funds, fixed-income exposure and even hybrid investments, which take a multi-asset approach between both equity and fixed-income. In addition to that, CEFs can take a specific sector exposure or a hybrid exposure to multiple sectors.

From there, it is important to note that not all premiums are necessarily a negative for a CEF, which is similar to the idea that not all discounts necessarily mean that a CEF is worth buying-even when they are trading at deep discounts of 40%+ on some occasions. That is, when one can invest in a fund at 60 cents on the dollar, it doesn't mean that it is a worthwhile investment.

Instead, we look for opportunities for CEFs that trade at wider-than-usual discounts or funds trading at relatively lower premiums compared to their history. That means that on some occasions, that can even include funds trading at a premium, which means the share price is above the NAV per share of the underlying portfolio.

As we enter 2025 and even cross through the first month (time moves fast when we are having fun!), it can be appropriate to look at overall valuations in the CEF space. In this case, we are unfortunately looking at discounts narrowing broadly across the space. Some funds are pushing into historically elevated discount/premium territory-and as we'll see below, the fixed-income space has really started to become quite elevated, historically speaking. This is a reversal from 2022 and 2023 when we were looking at historically wide discounts.

The market has delivered back-to-back solid gains into 2023 and 2024, which was after what was a fairly volatile 2022. This was particularly true for the mega-cap tech space and, even more specifically, the Magnificent 7 names.

With that, the broader market has simultaneously pushed CEFs to trade at historically narrow discounts now. This is a bit of a phenomenon where the broader overall market seems to influence CEFs. CEFs tend to be largely purchased by retail investors.

That doesn't mean that CEFs are devoid entirely of institutional investors, but some limitations can occur. The size of CEFs is relatively smaller, which has an influence on the daily trading volume. Being "closed" means that new shares aren't so easily issued as they can be with their exchange-traded fund counterparts-though there are ways to grow the overall outstanding shares through dividend reinvestment plans, at-the-market offerings, secondary offerings and rights offerings. These can both be accretive and/or dilutive, which depends on whether shares are issued at premiums or discounts, though that is a different subject for another time.

With that said, an institutional investor is often a larger entity managing billions in total capital, and for those reasons, it can limit participation from these larger investors when most CEFs are sub-$1 billion AUM. They could have a massive influence and easily become controlling shareholders as their stakes become majority holdings. That can also work for activist groups; as an example, Saba Capital Management can often garner significant influence for not all that much capital being deployed. However, trying to buy and sell such large blocks and be difficult due to liquidity constraints.

Therefore, these types of securities are mostly influenced by retail investors. Retail investors tend to get overly enthusiastic when times are good, but then turn to selling based on emotion when times are tough. Even while that should probably be the opposite of how investors should respond in the different scenarios, Warren Buffett's: "I will tell you how to become rich. Close the doors. Be fearful when others are greedy. Be greedy when others are fearful."

Simply put, it is much easier emotionally to invest in something when the price keeps rising (i.e., others are being greedy) compared to when investors are continuing to sell as a share price keeps declining (i.e., investors are being fearful.)

Given that, it comes to where we are in closed-end funds overall today, where the discounts have narrowed materially in the last year, and that has continued into 2025. Though we aren't that deep into 2025 yet, valuations for CEFs have remained on a narrowing trend more broadly, and that could mean tempering expectations going forward.

On average, all CEFs have seen discounts wider 46% of the time at the end of January 31, 2025, according to RiverNorth's data. That could otherwise actually suggest there could be plenty of areas of investing opportunity left.

However, discounts have narrowed even further since just the end of 2024 after we saw them declining through the year. As of February 10th, 2025, closing data, the average discount has come down to -2.44%.

This tightening of discounts was even more dramatic when we looked at the average discount at the end of 2023, which was -9.6%. That makes it all much more of a dramatic shift over the last year.

In looking at the average discount/premium level based on asset types of CEFs, equity is showing that, on average, there is a bit less value out there. In fact, as of February 10, 2025, they actually show an average discount -1.13%.

Worth noting is that one of the big outliers here is the Destiny Tech 100 (DXYZ), which can be really skewing the data with its nearly 900% premium listed on CEFConnect. Though worth noting is that they only provide a NAV quarterly.

Then where some opportunities might be in the multi-sector or hybrid allocation funds. On average, the discounts in this category comes in at -9.08%. That can include funds such as Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT), Nuveen Multi-Asset Income (NMAI) and Calamos Global Dynamic Income Fund (CHW), all trading at fairly attractive discount levels.

There is also Bexil Investment Trust (OTCPK:BXSY), they are listed as a hybrid but is almost entirely invested in equities. That fund trades at a deep discount, pushing out to nearly 38%, which can really skew the discount of the hybrid listed funds. BXSY is an interesting one, though some of the discounts could be considered deserved, as we covered previously. There is also the Highland Global Allocation Fund (HGLB) listed in the hybrid category and coming in at a discount that is similarly deep, pushing to a nearly 37% level.

With that said, in the taxable fixed-income space, we are seeing even more elevated valuations across the board as well. Going back to 1996, discounts have been wider 77% of the time. Making them historically quite richly valued. The average discount of the latest data on February 10, 2025, comes in at a similarly shallow -0.42%.

Of course, in the fixed-income space, we have the PIMCO funds, which notoriously skew the data due to regularly trading at premiums. This isn't all that unusual, so it isn't necessarily a negative-based on the 1-year z-score, some of these are even looking relatively attractive.

Name Ticker Premium/Discount 1-Year Z-score
PIMCO Income Strategy Fund II (PFN) 5.26% 1.1
PIMCO Income Strategy (PFL) 4.56% 0.32
PIMCO High Income (PHK) 5.30% -0.03
PIMCO Global StocksPLUS & Income (PGP) 0.50% -0.51
PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX) -11.60% -0.66
PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) 6.23% 1.59
PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) 14.05% 0.87
PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy (PCN) 15.34% -0.18
PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) 25.89% 0.03
PIMCO Access Income (PAXS) 6.09% 1

That said, we do have the more hybrid allocation fund PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX), which does offer a hefty discount. This fund is still more of a hybrid fund due to its heavy focus on energy investments, primarily Venture Global (VG) being a huge position.

While equities and taxable fixed-income are looking relatively more expensive, muni funds are looking like the best value when we look at the long-term history. However, in looking at the current average discount, it has narrowed over the last month when compared to the 2024 end. More recently, the average discount has moved to -6.95%-still a relative bargain.

That doesn't mean that there aren't still some pockets of opportunity or that CEFs will more broadly perform poorly in terms of total returns. As we noted, it isn't appropriate to put CEFs in a small box; as a single type of asset or sector, they can be invested in various areas of the market, and the underlying portfolio is important to be aware of.

That said, on an average basis, it could become more difficult going forward for CEF investors because of the historical narrowing of CEFs across the board. With that, the main takeaway is trying to be more selective when investing in CEFs overall.

Conclusion

CEFs often deliver relatively higher distribution rates to investors who choose to invest in these investment structures. CEFs can provide significant opportunities, but there are some meaningful differences that investors should familiarize themselves with that aren't always represented in other investment structures. While CEFs aren't representative of asset classes or sectors themselves specifically, each fund can be individually focused on different areas, meaning each CEF investment can perform quite differently.

In general, today, we looked to highlight why investors should be more picky about where they are choosing to put capital. We previously highlighted what risks there are in CEFs more broadly before investing in this space. We've seen discounts narrow across the board, which means that investors can't just blindly throw darts at a board. Being more patient or more selective in where an investor chooses to put capital to work in CEFs has a better chance of performing better going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
